enrichment.summary: Kyle responds to a request about Greyhound buses, sharing his personal experience riding one back from a cross-country bike trip due to a knee injury, his observations about the socioeconomic demographics of Greyhound riders, and an extensive comparison of bus systems across many countries he has traveled through. He closes with an opinion that city buses are bad for urban transportation and subways or cycling should replace them.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Predominantly poor people, a level of poverty he wasn't exposed to growing up
American buses are basic and not luxurious compared to Argentina or Baltic countries
Impressive — nearly plane-like with reclining seats, TVs, double-decker options
Terrifying and dangerous — drivers go too fast on mountain cliff roads
Bad idea — dangerous for cyclists, slow traffic; cities need underground subways instead
Bikes are an easy and healthy substitute for city buses; riding will fix being too out of shape to ride

transcript.text: Hello, Vidary Quest.
Steve from Tucson wanted to know,
I was wondering if you could talk about the Greyhound bus line and your experiences.
I have ridden Greyhound buses lines before, a few times sporadically.
I remember one time I rode one in a college spring break.
I rode my bike from Durango to San Diego in five and a half days.
From Cortez, which is right outside of Durango, to San Diego in five days.
I was going to ride there and back, but I got there and then the final day as I was getting there,
My knee was really hurting and I didn't think I'd be able to make it home in time before finals started.
Because my knee was just thrashed, so I took the bus back.
And the guy says, hey!
He does this introduction and his wife was riding along with him the whole ride.
I don't know if she did that a lot, but he's like, it's my wife.
And he's like, if somebody causes a scene, I'll kick you off, man.
No questions asked.
I think, yeah, the people you see riding Greyhounds are like poor.
It's like a level of poor that I wasn't exposed to growing up.
When I first, you know, I was younger and I got in those, I'd look around and be like, man, like, there's poor.
Like, you can just tell, like, there's poor people, man.
Like, old clothes and, like, I guess they cut their own hair.
I don't know.
It's hard to describe.
I can tell people are poor.
But, yeah, a lot of, like, black people.
I remember when I rode, I think I rode one out to Virginia.
And, yeah.
Anyways.
Yeah, bus, that's interesting you asked this question because I have a lot of experience riding buses in other countries.
Nepal, I think Indonesia.
Because I like bike tour around, but a lot of times when I bike tour, I, like, all of a sudden I'll get sick of bike touring.
Or I don't like a certain section, so I'll just throw my bike on the bus roof or whatever inside the bus.
And then I'll ride the bus and then you bike tour again.
But, um, so I've ridden buses in, like, all the countries I've traveled through, you know.
So I know that how buses are different throughout the world.
Buses in America aren't that luxurious.
We have bathrooms, but that's about it.
Yeah, I got a bus in the bathroom and, you know, it's pretty basic.
It's good enough, but in Argentina, they take that stuff real seriously.
They have, like, reclining seats you can, like, turn into beds pretty much.
You get your own personal TV to watch movies with.
I mean, this is in, like, 2001 when I was doing this.
Um, 13 years ago, though.
Um, well, no, not your own.
I think so, maybe.
No, no, not that, but.
But, yeah, I mean, they were, like, up and down.
It was, like, a plane, you know.
Like, it was, like, on the aisle, and there were lots of them.
If I remember.
But, yeah, and they're new, and they're double-decker, one of them.
And they don't have a lot of people, too.
Like, the buses in America are usually pretty jam-packed, but they're kind of empty.
In Argentina, this is, they're having economic problems, 2001.
But, um, Chile is pretty much the same as America.
Same kind of bus as America.
Um, in Bolivia, they're totally overcrowded.
And old.
Like, from the 70s and way up.
They're, like, old-school buses, and they're overcrowded.
Um, and, uh, Peru, I can't remember.
Peru's probably, like, Bolivia in that way.
Um, maybe even Ecuador.
But, uh, I rode a bus in, uh, uh, Lithuania.
From Lithuania to Latvia.
Lithuania to Latvia.
No, from Lithuania all the way to Riga, and then Riga to Estonia.
Yeah, yeah, all through the Baltics.
And, um, they, um, those buses were nice, too.
They had internet on the bus.
I think you had to pay extra for it.
So I didn't have it in my place.
But they had internet.
And, uh, um, and then you also had TVs that they showed you.
And, uh, yeah, it was nice.
And, uh, oh, and it was, like, I was in the, they had the seats facing each other with a table in the middle.
And I had the whole area to myself.
I felt like it was in a limo.
Um, yeah.
And, uh, yeah, in Nepal, they're really cheap and overcrowded.
And, um, and they're always, oh, yeah, and in Guatemala, too.
They drive way too fast in Guatemala.
I was scared.
Uh, I was terrified.
I, I, uh, climbed the highest mountain in Central America with a couple buddies.
And then we rode the, um, bus from some town in the north all the way back to the lake at Atlanta on the sides of mountains.
And the, the tires were actually screeching around most of the corners on the edge of the corner of the cliff.
Because this guy was going, like, 60 miles an hour.
And I was just praying that I wasn't going to die.
I mean, it was like, and I was, like, bowing.
I would never get in a bus there again.
And while I was there, too, those buses crashed into each other and, like, 60 people died.
I was there on the San Pedro and I watched as they brought all the bodies up and they had a big procession.
Like, the next day I was on acid and I was, like, I felt an earthquake.
And then I heard, like, all these people wailing, like, in a procession.
And that was the, that was the big funeral for all the people.
Um, but, uh, um, what else?
Where else?
I was on a city bus in Creole once and, uh, the guy stopped and, like, got out and talked to one of his friends in a car in the middle of traffic in the middle of the city for, like, five minutes.
And all these people stood on the bus.
Nobody said anything.
Oh, and in Chile, in the city buses, they actually race each other.
I was on one bus and there were two of them racing each other.
And, um, their boat would go from, like, 60 miles an hour down a, you know, a normal city road.
And, because I guess the guy in front gets all the, gets all the passengers, you know, and they're not organized enough to have them spaced out.
They're all, like, each, each bus driver owns their own bus and so they compete with each other.
And they drive so fast.
So, like, they're foreign.
They're all race car drivers, basically.
And they accelerate.
And those buses can accelerate and decelerate really fast.
And, uh, you have to race them.
Like, when you're biking to commuting around San Diego, you always have to race the buses because they're always, like, they're always stopping.
Like, every half long, they're stopping.
There's zillions of them.
Um, oh, my God.
Um, so, uh, let's see.
Oops, sorry.
I'm kind of high right now.
So, let's see.
Yeah, that's my story about buses, what I think about buses.
I don't like buses in cities.
We need, that was a bad idea.
Whoever allowed buses in cities was dumb.
They need to, we need to have underground subways in cities.
Buses just, they're dangerous for cyclists who have to get out on the road to avoid the buses and traffic.
They're always running people over.
Uh, they slow up all the cars behind them.
Buses are bad, man.
We need, we need, uh, we need subways.
Um, okay.
And I guess, you know, if you can't do buses, why don't people ride their bikes around, you know?
It's easy.
Even if, and if you're too fat to ride a bike around, if you ride your bike around, you won't be fat anymore.
It's the beauty of it.
Okay, peace out.
Bye.
